sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A study of first-year immigrant college students measured acculturation strategy and stress during the first semester. Students were grouped as integration-oriented, assimilation-oriented, separation-oriented, or marginalization-oriented based on survey responses about heritage culture and host-campus participation. Mean stress scores were 5.8, 6.7, 6.4, and 8.1 on a 1–10 scale, respectively. Integration-oriented students reported the highest perceived social support from both family and campus peers.
questions.stem: Which acculturation group had the highest mean stress score?

explanations.B: Correct. The marginalization-oriented group had a stress score of 8.1, higher than integration at 5.8, assimilation at 6.7, and separation at 6.4.
explanations.A: The passage does not support integration-oriented students; it points instead to marginalization-oriented students.
explanations.C: The passage does not support assimilation-oriented students; it points instead to marginalization-oriented students.
explanations.D: The passage does not support separation-oriented students; it points instead to marginalization-oriented students.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean first-semester stress scores by acculturation strategy.

questions.stem: Why might integration be linked to lower stress?

choices.A: It may preserve heritage support while also building host-campus ties
choices.B: It requires abandoning all prior identity
choices.C: It prevents contact with campus peers

explanations.A: Correct. Integration involves maintaining heritage identity while participating in the new setting, and the passage notes high support from both family and peers.
explanations.B: The passage does not support it requires abandoning all prior identity; it points instead to it may preserve heritage support while also building host-campus ties.
explanations.C: The passage does not support it prevents contact with campus peers; it points instead to it may preserve heritage support while also building host-campus ties.
explanations.D: The passage does not support it eliminates every academic stressor; it points instead to it may preserve heritage support while also building host-campus ties.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.C: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.B: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.D: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.C: Correct. Students were grouped by existing acculturation responses rather than assigned to strategies, so language fluency, discrimination, or finances could contribute.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.B: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.D: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.D: Support cultural maintenance while increasing meaningful campus connection
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.B: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.C: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.D: Correct. The lower-stress integration pattern suggests students may benefit from both heritage support and host-campus belonging.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.B: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.C: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.C: Acculturation strategy was associated with stress differences, with integration showing the lowest stress
choices.A: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.B: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.C: Correct. This conclusion uses the observed pattern without claiming that strategy alone caused every difference.
explanations.A: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.B: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.

questions.passage: A community survey compared willingness to seek counseling before and after a culturally tailored mental-health workshop. The workshop included testimonials from community members, explanations of confidentiality, and discussion of how distress can be addressed while preserving family dignity. Before the workshop, 32% of respondents said they would consider counseling. Afterward, 54% said they would consider counseling. The largest change occurred among respondents who initially believed counseling would bring shame to the family.
questions.stem: What happened to willingness to consider counseling after the workshop?

explanations.B: Correct. The passage reports an increase from 32% before the workshop to 54% afterward, a 22-point gain in willingness.
explanations.A: The passage does not support it decreased from 54% to 32%; it points instead to it increased from 32% to 54%.
explanations.C: The passage does not support it stayed exactly the same; it points instead to it increased from 32% to 54%.
explanations.D: The passage does not support it dropped to zero; it points instead to it increased from 32% to 54%.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Percent willing to consider counseling before and after a culturally tailored workshop.

questions.stem: Why might testimonials from community members reduce stigma?

choices.A: They can make help-seeking seem socially acceptable within the group

explanations.A: Correct. Testimonials can shift perceived norms by showing that relatable community members view counseling as acceptable.
explanations.B: The passage does not support they replace all professional training; it points instead to they can make help-seeking seem socially acceptable within the group.
explanations.C: The passage does not support they prove confidentiality is unnecessary; it points instead to they can make help-seeking seem socially acceptable within the group.
explanations.D: The passage does not support they make symptoms imaginary; it points instead to they can make help-seeking seem socially acceptable within the group.

questions.stem: Why should causal language be used cautiously for this passage?

choices.D: The design leaves room for alternative explanations or selection effects
choices.A: The passage contains no measurable variables
choices.B: All participant reports are automatically false
choices.C: The topic is unrelated to P/S content

explanations.D: Correct. Without a comparison group, some of the pre/post change could reflect time, demand characteristics, or other influences besides the workshop.
explanations.A: The passage includes concrete measures or observations, so this is incorrect.
explanations.B: Self-report can have limitations, but it is not automatically false.
explanations.C: The topic fits psychological, social, or behavioral science content.

questions.stem: Which application best follows from the passage?

choices.B: Address confidentiality, stigma, and community-specific concerns directly
choices.A: Ignore the social or behavioral context described
choices.C: Use a one-size-fits-all intervention even when the passage shows different barriers
choices.D: Assume the measured pattern cannot be changed

explanations.B: Correct. The workshop improved willingness by targeting the specific beliefs that made counseling seem socially costly.
explanations.A: Ignoring the described context would miss the mechanism emphasized in the passage.
explanations.C: A uniform intervention would not address the specific pattern described.
explanations.D: The passage supports practical response rather than fatalism.

choices.A: A culturally tailored workshop was followed by greater counseling willingness, especially among those worried about family shame
choices.B: The finding proves a universal law for every person
choices.C: The main construct is irrelevant because one limitation exists
choices.D: The passage supports only biological explanations

explanations.A: Correct. This conclusion captures the observed change while remaining cautious about causal certainty.
explanations.B: The passage supports a bounded interpretation, not a universal absolute.
explanations.C: A limitation weakens overclaiming but does not make the construct irrelevant.
explanations.D: P/S passages often involve social and psychological mechanisms beyond biology alone.
